ai_summary_bullets.text: Knowledge graph-enhanced recommendation suffers from noisy KG triplets that introduce irrelevant entity connections, degrading the quality of learned representations. Standard KG-based methods like KGAT and KGIN propagate all KG relations uniformly, amplifying noise. Meanwhile, contrastive learning for recommendation (SGL, SimGCL) shows promise but doesn't leverage KG structure.

KGCL applies contrastive learning to KG-enhanced recommendation with a novel KG-guided augmentation strategy: it uses learned KG attention scores to identify and drop noisy edges (low-attention triplets) rather than random augmentation, creating semantically meaningful contrastive views. The framework combines a KG-aware GNN for information aggregation with cross-view contrastive objectives on both user-item and KG graphs.

KGCL improves over KGAT by 5-13% on Recall@20 across Last.FM, Alibaba-iFashion, and Amazon-Book datasets. The KG-guided augmentation consistently outperforms random augmentation (SGL-style), validating that noise-aware contrastive views are critical. Key interview insight: contrastive learning in rec systems benefits most when augmentation is semantically informed rather than random.

Session-based recommendation must capture complex item transitions from anonymous short sessions, but GNN-based methods (SR-GNN, GCE-GNN) only model pairwise item relations and miss higher-order correlations. For example, buying a phone, case, and screen protector together is a 3-way correlation that pairwise graphs cannot represent. Self-supervised signals are also underexplored in session recommendation.

DHCN uses hypergraph convolution to capture beyond-pairwise item correlations within sessions -- each session becomes a hyperedge connecting all its items. The model applies dual-channel hypergraph convolution (session-level and item-level channels) and introduces self-supervised learning via maximizing mutual information between the two hypergraph views using a contrastive objective, providing auxiliary supervision for sparse session data.

DHCN outperforms SR-GNN and GCE-GNN by 3-8% on Tmall, Retailrocket, and Nowplaying datasets in Hit@20 and MRR@20. The self-supervised component alone contributes 2-4% improvement, confirming contrastive learning helps with session data sparsity. Key insight for interviews: hypergraph structures capture group-level item affinities that simple graphs miss, particularly important for session/basket recommendation.

Summarization models trained with supervised learning on human-written references optimize for surface-level similarity metrics (ROUGE) that correlate poorly with actual summary quality. Models learn to extract and rearrange sentences rather than produce genuinely useful summaries. The gap between what ROUGE measures and what humans prefer creates a ceiling on summary quality achievable through standard training.

OpenAI trains a reward model on 64K human comparisons of summary pairs, then uses PPO to optimize a GPT-3-based summarizer against this learned reward signal. The key design: comparisons are collected at the summary level (not sentence level) from a carefully selected pool of labelers with high inter-annotator agreement. A KL penalty against the supervised baseline prevents reward hacking.

RLHF-trained models produce summaries preferred by humans over both the supervised baseline and the human-written reference summaries from the TL;DR dataset. This was the first compelling demonstration that RLHF could surpass supervised learning for a real NLP task and directly influenced the InstructGPT/ChatGPT line of work. Key insight: human preference optimization > maximum likelihood training when the evaluation metric doesn't fully capture quality.

The original Deep & Cross Network (DCN) uses a cross network to model explicit feature interactions, but its cross layers are limited in expressiveness -- they can only learn a specific family of cross functions parameterized by a single weight vector per layer. This limits the model's ability to capture complex feature interactions needed for CTR prediction at scale.

DCN V2 replaces the vector-based cross layer with a matrix-based one (CrossNet-v2), where each layer uses a full weight matrix W instead of a vector, enabling it to model all pairwise feature crosses. It also introduces a mixture-of-experts variant (DCN-Mix) that applies multiple cross experts in a gated manner. The paper provides extensive practical lessons from deploying at Google: low-rank approximation of W for efficiency, and a stacked vs. parallel architecture study.

DCN V2 achieves 0.1-0.3% AUC improvement over DCN on Criteo and production Google Ads data, which translates to significant revenue impact at scale. DCN-Mix provides the best accuracy-efficiency trade-off. The paper's practical production lessons (feature embedding dimension tuning, cross-layer depth analysis, serving latency constraints) make it essential reading for CTR system design interviews. Deployed at Google for ad CTR prediction.

Building AI assistants requires balancing helpfulness (following user instructions well) with harmlessness (refusing harmful requests). These objectives often conflict: a maximally helpful model will assist with harmful requests, while an overly cautious model refuses benign ones. Prior RLHF work focused on helpfulness alone, without systematically addressing the helpfulness-harmlessness trade-off.

Anthropic collects separate preference datasets for helpfulness and harmlessness through red-teaming, then trains distinct reward models for each dimension. The RLHF training uses a combination of both reward signals with careful weighting. The paper introduces the concept of a Pareto frontier between helpfulness and harmlessness, showing how different reward weightings trace out this frontier. They also study the effect of model scale on alignment.

RLHF training produces models that are both more helpful AND more harmless than the base model, finding Pareto improvements. Larger models benefit more from RLHF and show better helpfulness-harmlessness trade-offs. This work established the framework for multi-objective alignment that Anthropic uses for Claude and influenced the field's understanding that alignment is a multi-dimensional optimization problem, not a single objective.

LLMs with chain-of-thought prompting generate reasoning in a left-to-right autoregressive manner, which is fundamentally greedy -- they cannot look ahead, backtrack, or plan. This fails on tasks requiring strategic planning, exploration, or multi-step decision-making where early choices constrain later options (e.g., Blocksworld, Game of 24).

RAP (Reasoning via Planning) repurposes the LLM as both a world model (predicting next states) and a reasoning agent, then applies Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to explore the reasoning space. The LLM simulates future states and provides reward signals, while MCTS balances exploration vs exploitation using UCB scores. This enables backtracking and look-ahead without any additional training.

RAP achieves 73% on Blocksworld (vs 2% for CoT) and 66% on Game of 24 (vs 15% for CoT), demonstrating that planning-based decoding dramatically improves LLM reasoning on tasks requiring search. The compute cost is ~10-40x more inference FLOPs than CoT. Key insight for interviews: LLM reasoning can be improved at test time by treating generation as a search problem, trading compute for accuracy -- a theme echoed in OpenAI's o1 and similar work.

Complex LLM programs that combine generation with control flow (branching, loops, tool calls, structured output) are inefficient to execute because each component runs as a separate API call with redundant KV cache computation. Frameworks like LangChain treat each generation step independently, losing opportunities for batching, parallelism, and KV cache reuse across program components.

SGLang introduces a domain-specific language for structured LLM programs and a runtime that co-optimizes program execution with the inference engine. Key techniques: RadixAttention (a radix tree-based KV cache management that enables automatic prefix sharing across requests), compressed finite state machines for constrained decoding, and speculative scheduling that overlaps generation with control flow. The frontend uses Python-embedded primitives (fork, select, gen) that compile into optimized execution plans.

SGLang achieves 3-6x speedup over LangChain/Guidance for multi-turn agents, JSON-constrained generation, and tree-of-thought programs. RadixAttention alone provides 2x throughput improvement for chat workloads by reusing system prompt KV cache. Adopted by major LLM serving providers and integrated with vLLM. Essential for interviews on LLM serving infrastructure -- understanding KV cache sharing and structured generation optimization.

Pruning LLMs is fundamentally different from pruning CNNs because (1) LLMs have complex inter-layer dependencies where removing one attention head affects all downstream computations, (2) the cost of retraining after pruning is prohibitive for 7B+ models, and (3) standard unstructured pruning (SparseGPT) doesn't translate to actual wall-clock speedups without specialized hardware.

LLM-Pruner performs structured (not unstructured) pruning by identifying and removing coupled groups of weights that form functional units (attention heads, FFN neurons). It uses gradient-based importance estimation to rank these groups, then removes the least important ones. Post-pruning recovery uses LoRA fine-tuning on a small dataset (~50K samples, ~3 hours on 1 GPU) instead of full retraining, making it practical for large models.

Removes 20% of LLaMA-7B parameters with only 2-3% accuracy degradation on zero-shot benchmarks, achieving real 1.3x inference speedup. At 50% pruning, accuracy drops more significantly but can be partially recovered with LoRA. Key interview insight: structured pruning + lightweight recovery (LoRA) is more practical than unstructured pruning for LLM deployment because it yields actual speedups on standard hardware without custom sparse kernels.

Multi-step reasoning with retrieval (like multi-hop QA) faces a bootstrapping problem: the question alone doesn't contain enough information to retrieve all necessary documents upfront. Standard retrieve-then-read retrieves based on the original question and misses documents needed for later reasoning steps. Doing all retrieval first leads to irrelevant results; doing all reasoning first leads to hallucinated intermediate facts.

IRCoT interleaves chain-of-thought reasoning with retrieval: after each CoT step, it uses the generated sentence as a query to retrieve relevant documents, then continues reasoning conditioned on both the accumulated CoT and newly retrieved evidence. This creates a virtuous cycle where each reasoning step informs better retrieval, and better retrieval grounds the next reasoning step.

IRCoT improves over one-step retrieval + CoT by 8-15% on HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA, and MuSiQue (multi-hop QA benchmarks) across GPT-3.5, Codex, and Flan-T5. The approach is model-agnostic and works with any retriever. Key insight for RAG system design: for multi-step tasks, interleaving retrieval and generation outperforms both retrieve-all-then-reason and reason-then-retrieve approaches.

Recommendation systems suffer from multiple biases -- popularity bias (popular items get recommended more, creating feedback loops), exposure bias (users only interact with shown items), and conformity bias. Existing debiasing methods like IPS require knowing the propensity scores, which are hard to estimate accurately, and inverse propensity weighting has high variance.

AutoDebias learns debiasing parameters automatically through a meta-learning framework: it uses a small uniform-exposure dataset (collected via random recommendation) as a validation set, and optimizes the debiasing parameters on the training set such that the model performs well on the unbiased validation set. This avoids explicitly estimating propensity scores and automatically adapts to the specific bias distribution in the data.

AutoDebias outperforms IPS, SNIPS, and CausE by 3-8% on Yahoo R3 and Coat datasets (which have unbiased test sets), while being more robust to propensity estimation errors. Key insight for rec system interviews: meta-learning-based debiasing is more practical than explicit propensity estimation because it automatically adapts to unknown bias distributions, and a small random-traffic dataset is sufficient for calibration.

Sequential recommendation models (SASRec, BERT4Rec) primarily rely on item IDs for sequence modeling, but items have rich side information (category, brand, price) that is underutilized. Naively concatenating side information with item embeddings leads to feature interaction interference -- the model conflates positional/sequential signals with attribute signals, degrading both.

DIF-SR (Decoupled Side Information Fusion) proposes generating separate attention scores for different types of information and fusing them at the attention level rather than the embedding level. Each side information type has its own attention computation, and the final attention is a learned fusion of these parallel attention streams. This preserves the independence of sequential patterns and attribute patterns while enabling cross-information flow.

DIF-SR improves SASRec by 5-12% on Amazon Beauty, Sports, and Yelp in NDCG@10 and Hit@10, with consistent gains when added to other sequential models (BERT4Rec, GRU4Rec). Key interview insight: in sequential recommendation, the way you fuse different information types matters more than what you fuse -- attention-level fusion preserves signal independence better than embedding-level concatenation.

Multi-behavior recommendation (modeling click, add-to-cart, purchase, etc.) requires capturing behavior-specific patterns and cross-behavior dependencies. Existing methods either treat behaviors independently (missing transfer signals) or mix them uniformly (losing behavior-specific nuances). Graph-based methods struggle with long-range cross-behavior dependencies.

KHGT combines knowledge graphs with a hierarchical graph transformer for multi-behavior modeling. It constructs behavior-specific user-item graphs and a shared KG, then applies graph transformers with behavior-aware attention to capture both within-behavior patterns and cross-behavior knowledge transfer. The hierarchical design processes local behavior graphs first, then fuses representations across behaviors.

KHGT outperforms MBGCN and GNMR by 3-7% on Tmall and Beibei datasets. The KG integration helps most for auxiliary behaviors (clicks, views) that have richer KG connections. Key insight for rec interviews: multi-behavior recommendation benefits from hierarchical fusion -- model each behavior separately first, then transfer learned patterns across behaviors, rather than mixing all behaviors from the start.

Multi-behavior recommendation data is heterogeneous -- users have different behavior patterns (some browse a lot but buy little, others buy directly) and behaviors have different sparsity levels. Standard models apply the same architecture to all users and all behaviors, failing to adapt to this heterogeneity. Meta-learning can help personalize, but existing meta-rec methods don't handle multiple behavior types.

Graph Meta Network (GMN) uses meta-learning to generate personalized model parameters for each user-behavior combination. It constructs a multi-behavior interaction graph, then uses a meta-network that takes user/behavior context as input and generates customized GNN aggregation weights. This allows the model to learn different aggregation strategies for different users and different behaviors simultaneously.

GMN outperforms MAML-based and graph-based baselines by 4-9% on Tmall and Beibei for purchase prediction. The meta-learned parameters show meaningful variation across users -- heavy browsers get different aggregation weights than direct purchasers. Key insight: in multi-behavior recommendation, personalizing the model architecture per user-behavior pair via meta-learning captures heterogeneity that shared-parameter models miss.

Two-tower models (dual encoders) are the dominant architecture for large-scale retrieval at companies like Google, Meta, and YouTube, but they suffer from position bias and selection bias in training data -- users interact with items that were shown to them, not all possible items. Standard training on biased click data leads to models that perpetuate existing ranking biases rather than learning true relevance.

This work applies unbiased learning-to-rank (ULTR) techniques specifically to the two-tower retrieval setting. It incorporates inverse propensity scoring and position-bias correction directly into the contrastive/softmax loss used for training dual encoders, adapting techniques originally designed for pointwise ranking losses to the pairwise/listwise setting of retrieval models.

Debiased two-tower models show significant improvement in top-K retrieval metrics compared to naive training on biased clicks. Key interview insight for retrieval system design: two-tower models at scale always train on biased interaction data (position bias, selection bias), and applying ULTR corrections during training is essential for learning true relevance rather than memorizing the previous ranker's preferences.

SASRec (Self-Attentive Sequential Recommendation) and its variants are overconfident in their predictions -- they assign near-1.0 probabilities to top-ranked items, which hurts calibration and makes the model unable to express uncertainty. This overconfidence stems from the combination of dot-product scoring with cross-entropy loss, which pushes logits to extreme values during training.

gSASRec (generalized SASRec) identifies that SASRec's overconfidence comes from two sources: (1) the unbounded dot-product similarity score, and (2) insufficient regularization. It proposes clipping logits, using dropout on attention weights, and temperature scaling at inference. More importantly, it provides a rigorous re-evaluation of SASRec with proper hyperparameter tuning, showing that many reported improvements over SASRec were actually due to unfair comparisons.

A properly tuned SASRec baseline is much stronger than previously reported, closing the gap with many claimed improvements. gSASRec's calibration fixes further improve NDCG by 2-5% on Amazon and MovieLens datasets. Key insight for rec interviews: many sequential recommendation papers overclaim improvements due to weak baselines -- always verify baseline implementations, and model calibration (not just ranking accuracy) matters for production systems.

Late interaction models like ColBERT store per-token embeddings for each document, enabling fine-grained matching but requiring massive storage (hundreds of GB for web-scale corpora). Original ColBERT also only supports English. Scaling late interaction to multilingual settings while keeping storage manageable is an open challenge.

Jina-ColBERT-v2 combines a multilingual backbone (XLM-RoBERTa) with aggressive embedding compression: it uses linear projection to reduce token embedding dimensions from 768 to 128, applies residual quantization to compress each vector to 2 bytes, and uses centroid-based indexing for efficient retrieval. Training uses knowledge distillation from a cross-encoder teacher and hard negative mining.

Achieves competitive retrieval quality with ColBERT-v2 on English BEIR benchmarks while supporting 100+ languages. Storage is reduced 4-8x compared to original ColBERT. Key insight for search interviews: late interaction (per-token matching) provides better relevance than bi-encoder but requires careful compression engineering for production deployment -- the trade-off between matching granularity and storage/latency is a core system design consideration.
